name: Rainforest Alliance
mission: The Rainforest Alliance works to encourage better on-the-ground forestry practices by rewarding businesses, communities and governments that meet standards for sustainability.
theoryOfChange: The Rainforest Alliance has a published "Theory of Change for the 2020 Certification Program" which outlines the results chain logic, including outputs, short-term outcomes, intermediate outcomes, long-term outcomes, and impacts. This framework is used to design projects and programs, incorporating indicators to track outcomes and impacts, such as their indicator framework for the Rainforest Alliance Certification Program. The Theory of Change also details complementary strategies that enhance the enabling environment to contribute to overall impact.
scoreSummary: RAINFOREST ALLIANCE shows strong impact and good alignment.

evaluationSources: COSA evaluation of UTZ certification in Ghana
Institute of Social Development evaluation of tea program in Sri Lanka

ui_signals_v1.recommendation_rationale: Good donor alignment with manageable risk. Evidence quality is solid but still evolving in places.

keyConcerns.headline: CEO compensation is 1.4% of revenue ($1,419,409 on $100,221,940)
keyConcerns.detail: For orgs with revenue >$50M, CEO pay above 1% of revenue warrants scrutiny.

components.improvement_suggestion: Add a dedicated zakat page or clearly state whether donations are zakat-eligible.

components.evidence: No beneficiary data available for cost-per-beneficiary scoring; good program ratio (≥75%) suggests reasonable delivery
components.improvement_suggestion: Publish beneficiary counts consistently so cost-effectiveness can be assessed.

judge_issues.message: Website citation for 'claims_zakat_eligible' uses homepage URL; deep link preferred

risks.description: CEO compensation is 1.4% of revenue ($1,419,409 on $100,221,940)

score_details.score_summary: RAINFOREST ALLIANCE shows strong impact and good alignment.

zakat.claim_evidence: Organization is in zakat denylist (partners but doesn't collect directly) (Source: https://rainforest-alliance.org)

all_citations.claim: The organization reported a total revenue of $100,221,940 and a program expense ratio of 83.5%.
Revenue includes significant contributions from royalty fees on certified products.
The organization accepts general charitable donations (Sadaqah) to support its environmental mission.
The charity holds a 96.5/100 score, with a fundraising efficiency of $0.04 per $1 raised and 7.8 months of working capital.

baseline_narrative.amal_score_rationale: The organization earns a solid overall rating due to its exceptional financial health and high accountability score of 96.96.5/100 from Charity Navigator. While its capacity-building model is effective, there is room to improve direct impact evidence for individual beneficiaries.
areas_for_improvement: Enhancing direct impact measurement beyond capacity building metrics
Increasing alignment with specific donor demographics through targeted outreach

dimension_explanations.alignment: As a Sadaqah-eligible organization, it offers a medium fit for donors interested in environmental advocacy and sustainable development [4]. The mission addresses urgent global climate and livelihood issues, though it does not offer specific religious-based giving pathways [4][5].
dimension_explanations.impact: The organization demonstrates high financial health and transparency, maintaining a 96.5/100 rating from Charity Navigator [5]. Its impact model focuses on capacity building within forestry and agriculture, ensuring that 83.5% of its $100,221,940 revenue is directed toward these core programs [1][2].

baseline_narrative.headline: The Rainforest Alliance fosters global sustainability by aligning the interests of businesses, governments, and forest communities to protect biodiversity and improve livelihoods.
strengths: High financial efficiency with a program expense ratio of 83.5%
Excellent fundraising efficiency, spending only $0.04 to raise every $1
Strong liquidity with 7.8 months of working capital available
baseline_narrative.summary: The Rainforest Alliance is a global non-profit dedicated to promoting sustainable forestry and agricultural practices through market-based rewards and rigorous standards [1][5]. In the 2023 fiscal year, the organization reported a total revenue of $100,221,940, driven largely by royalty revenue from certified products [1][2].

all_citations.quote: Programs: Sustainability Certification, Regenerative Agriculture, Landscape and Community Projects
Areas Served: Brazil, Mexico, Peru, Côte d'Ivoire, Nicaragua, Indonesia

all_citations.claim: 1.6 billion people who depend on forests
all_citations.quote: Roughly 1.6 billion people around the world directly depend on forests for their livelihoods.
Working Capital: 7.8 months of working capital

Platinum Seal of Transparency from Candid

all_citations.quote: Improvement: Publish beneficiary counts consistently so cost-effectiveness can be assessed.

rich_narrative.amal_score_rationale: The organization earns a solid overall rating due to its exceptional financial health and high accountability score of 96.96.5/100 from Charity Navigator. While its capacity-building model is effective, there is room to improve direct impact evidence for individual beneficiaries.

areas_for_improvement.context: While the organization tracks broad outcomes, it could <cite id="16">publish more consistent beneficiary counts</cite> to allow for detailed cost-per-beneficiary assessments.
The <cite id="17">CEO's total compensation of $464,962</cite> represents a significant portion of revenue for an organization of this scale, warranting closer board scrutiny.

bbb_assessment.summary: The organization currently does not meet any of the 20 BBB Standards for Charity Accountability.

case_against.mitigation_notes: The organization maintains high third-party ratings from Charity Navigator and Candid despite BBB reporting gaps.
risk_factors: <cite id="20">0 out of 20 BBB standards met</cite>
<cite id="17">High executive compensation relative to revenue</cite>
case_against.summary: The primary concerns involve <cite id="20">non-compliance with BBB standards</cite> and a delivery model focused on <cite id="18">capacity building rather than direct aid</cite>.

confidence.data_freshness: Financial data from 2023; ratings from late 2024
confidence.notes: Strong financial and transparency data available from multiple authoritative sources.

alignment.explanation: The charity serves <cite id="22">underserved populations in Muslim-majority regions</cite> like Indonesia and Côte d'Ivoire, though it lacks specific faith-based programming.
alignment.improvement: The organization could <cite id="23">add a dedicated sadaqah page</cite> to better communicate its alignment with Muslim donor priorities.

credibility.explanation: Donors can trust this charity due to its <cite id="12">perfect accountability scores</cite>, <cite id="13">Candid Platinum status</cite>, and <cite id="19">100% independent board of directors</cite>.
credibility.improvement: To further enhance credibility, the organization should work to <cite id="20">meet all 20 BBB Wise Giving Alliance standards</cite>, as current reviews show 0 standards met.

impact.explanation: Operations are highly efficient with a <cite id="3">83.5% program ratio</cite> and a <cite id="21">strong documented theory of change</cite>.
impact.improvement: Impact assessment would benefit from <cite id="16">publishing specific beneficiary data</cite> to move beyond capacity-building metrics.

donor_fit_matrix.evidence_rigor: Grade B - Strong theory of change with outcome tracking

grantmaking_profile.grant_strategy: Distributes significant funding to local partners for on-the-ground sustainability work.

rich_narrative.headline: The Rainforest Alliance fosters global sustainability by aligning the interests of businesses, governments, and forest communities to protect biodiversity and improve livelihoods.

ideal_donor_profile.best_for_summary: Best for donors who value market-driven environmental solutions and want to support long-term systemic change in global agriculture and forestry.
donor_motivations: Want to address climate change through sustainable supply chains
Value organizations with high financial efficiency and low fundraising costs
Prefer supporting rural livelihoods through capacity building
Interested in protecting biodiversity in tropical rainforests
giving_considerations: Donations support advocacy and certification rather than direct emergency relief
The organization has a large institutional footprint with high executive pay
Impact is measured through systemic changes rather than individual beneficiary counts

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: Grade B reflects a strong, documented theory of change and consistent tracking of outcome metrics through Candid.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: The organization uses market-based incentives to reward sustainable land management, thereby protecting forests and improving farmer livelihoods.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Evidence ensures that certification standards actually lead to improved environmental and economic outcomes for forest communities.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: The organization has a high revenue growth rate but also significant expenses related to its global certification infrastructure.

peer_comparison.differentiator: Distinguished by its <cite id="4">high program service revenue</cite> from certification royalties, reducing dependence on traditional donations.

strengths.detail: The organization directs <cite id="3">83.5% of its total expenses</cite> toward program services, significantly exceeding the <cite id="8">peer median of 81.3%</cite> for advocacy-focused organizations.
The charity spends only <cite id="9">$0.04 to raise every $1</cite> in contributions, reflecting a highly efficient development operation.
With <cite id="10">7.8 months of working capital</cite> in reserves, the organization maintains a strong liquidity position to weather economic fluctuations.

strengths_deep_dive: The Rainforest Alliance demonstrates a high level of organizational maturity and transparency, evidenced by its <cite id="11">overall score of 96.96.5/100 from Charity Navigator</cite>. This includes <cite id="12">perfect 96/100 scores</cite> in Accountability, Culture, and Leadership beacons. Their impact is further validated by a <cite id="13">Platinum Seal of Transparency from Candid</cite>, the highest level of disclosure available. Financially, the organization has shown rapid growth with a <cite id="14">3-year revenue CAGR of 24.9%</cite>, allowing for expanded grantmaking activity which totaled <cite id="15">$121,353,569 in grants</cite> to domestic and foreign partners in the most recent reporting period.
rich_narrative.summary: The Rainforest Alliance is a global non-profit organization that works at the intersection of business, agriculture, and forests to make responsible business the new normal. <cite id="1">founded in 1987</cite>, the organization has spent nearly four decades developing market-based solutions to protect biodiversity and improve the livelihoods of rural communities. In the 2023 fiscal year, the organization reported <cite id="2">total revenue of $85,534,243</cite>, demonstrating significant scale in its operations. The charity operates with high financial efficiency, maintaining a <cite id="3">program expense ratio of 83.5%</cite>, which ensures that the vast majority of funding is directed toward its core mission of sustainability. Their model is uniquely sustainable, generating <cite id="4">$57,766,882 in program service revenue</cite> primarily through royalty fees from certified products. This diversified income stream supports a wide range of initiatives, including <cite id="5">regenerative agriculture and landscape projects</cite> across critical regions such as <cite id="6">Brazil, Indonesia, and Côte d'Ivoire</cite>. By aligning the interests of <cite id="7">1.6 billion people who depend on forests</cite> with global market demands, the Rainforest Alliance creates a scalable framework for environmental conservation and climate resilience.
